items.q: How do hotspot volcanoes differ from subduction-zone volcanoes?
items.correct: Hotspots are stationary mantle plumes that punch through plates anywhere (e.g. Hawaii), while subduction-zone volcanoes form along plate boundaries from melting subducted crust.
items.misconception: Hotspot volcanoes form at plate boundaries like all other volcanoes.
items.random_wrong: Hotspot volcanoes are just very hot subduction volcanoes.
items.why: Hawaii sits in the middle of the Pacific Plate, nowhere near a boundary. The hotspot is fixed while the plate moves over it, producing volcanic chains — a different mechanism entirely.

items.correct: The sudden release of built-up stress along faults at plate boundaries as rocks slip past each other, generating seismic waves.
items.misconception: Earthquakes are caused by magma rising up to the surface.
items.random_wrong: Earthquakes are caused by the Earth's core overheating.
items.why: Students conflate volcanic and seismic processes. Most earthquakes are tectonic (stress release at faults), not volcanic — magma movement is a separate process and most quakes occur at non-volcanic boundaries.

items.q: How does an oxbow lake form from a meander?
items.correct: Erosion on outside bends narrows the meander neck; during a flood the river cuts across the neck, deposition seals the old loop, leaving a horseshoe-shaped lake.
items.misconception: Oxbow lakes form when meanders dry up completely.
items.random_wrong: Oxbow lakes form where two rivers meet and one stops flowing.
items.why: The lake remains water-filled — it's a cut-off loop, not a dry feature. Students miss that the active river abandons the meander after cutting through, and deposition isolates the old channel.
The answer examiners want is: Richter. “Mercalli” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: The San Andreas Fault is what type of margin?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Conservative. “Constructive” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: What was the magnitude of the 2010 Haiti earthquake?
items.why: Exact answer: 7 Richter. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
